schools.summary: The rest comes after the Philistine wars but before the foreign campaigns; the episode is placed by subject, in a season of quiet prosperity.
Matthew Henry: David's rest breeds not luxury but a grateful design to house the ark; gracious souls never think they have done enough for God.
Gill: David, settled in Hiram's palace with no war to wage, reflects on the unsettled state of the ark.
Wesley: now settled in his house, David turns from his own ease to the ark's want of a fixed dwelling.
'Within curtains' is the tabernacle's own word (Exodus 26); Nathan is already the king's trusted counsellor and a recorder of the history.
Gill: Nathan's first appearance, a man of piety and prophetic gift; the tradition identifying him with Jonathan ben Shimea is unlikely.
Wesley: the 'curtains' are the tent of hangings that housed the ark.

outliers.note: Records and rejects the Jewish tradition that Nathan the prophet was Jonathan the son of Shimea, David's nephew.

schools.summary: Gill: Nathan spoke as a private godly man; he and David erred in not consulting the Lord and were tacitly reproved.
Wesley and Clarke: the prophet approved hastily, as a prudent man not under inspiration; prophets were divinely moved only at select times.
Ellicott: Nathan judged by his own sense of right, not by divine direction, though the purpose was pious.

debates.question: Was Nathan's endorsement a fault or merely an uninspired human judgement?

sides.position: a real error, tacitly reproved and soon recanted
simply his own pious opinion, not spoken by divine direction

schools.summary: The word comes 'that night'; its flat contradiction of Nathan's own counsel authenticates it as external revelation, not the prophet's thought.
Matthew Henry: God sends the correction by the same hand and the same night, upholding the prophet's honour and sparing David a longer error.
Gill: the promptness prevents both men from continuing in mistake and checks David's warm zeal before it acts.

themes: God's regard for the good intention

schools.summary: The question means 'thou shalt not' (so 1 Chronicles 17:4, LXX, Syriac); 'my servant' shows the refusal implies no displeasure; the reason, unstated here, is David the man of blood (1 Chronicles 22:8).
Gill: God speaks honourably of David, owning him his servant and accepting his good will though declining to employ him in this.

debates.question: Why is David forbidden to build the temple?

sides.position: because he was a man of war who shed much blood (the reason given later, in Chronicles)
because God must first build David's house, and the kingdom was not yet at peace (a common inference, but not stated in the word)

schools.summary: Matthew Henry: God never complained of the tent; his presence needs no pomp, and David had sweeter communion in curtains than his heirs in cedar.
Keil: while his dwelling was a tent, God appeared as a traveller journeying among his people.
Wesley: 'tent' and 'tabernacle' distinguish the inner hangings from the outer frame of boards.
Gill: God had had no fixed, stated habitation from the Exodus until this day.
'Tribes' or 'judges'? The words differ by one Hebrew letter; Clarke prefers 'judges' (so Chronicles), Ellicott defends 'tribes' as standing for the judges.
Matthew Henry: only instituted worship is acceptable; God never commanded a temple, so David must wait for a warrant — better a tent of God's appointing than a temple of one's own inventing.
Gill: God had walked with his people in many places and never asked a house of any of them.

sides.position: 'judges' is the true reading (so 1 Chronicles 17:6)
'tribes' can stand, used for the judges sprung from them

schools.summary: 'Jehovah of hosts' now speaks; he took David from the pasture behind the flock to be prince, and went with him to cut off his foes and make his name great.
Gill: the recital of favours assures David that the refusal is no slight; it was honour enough to be raised from a low estate.
Wesley: the repeated title 'my servant' is given lest David feel neglected, signifying God's acceptance of his good intentions.
Gill: God's presence attended David against Goliath, against the Philistines, in Saul's court, and in his flight — everywhere protecting and prospering.
Keil: the perfect verbs of past mercy — with thee, cut off thy foes, made thy name great — become the ground of the coming promise of a house.
Matthew Henry: God gave both victory and a great name, yet a good name is more desirable than a great one, and all is the giver's doing.
Ellicott and Clarke: no change of tense — read 'have appointed, have planted'; the verse recites what God has now done, not a future prosperity.
Gill: the place is Canaan, appointed of old but now to be wholly possessed once the Canaanites are expelled.
Wesley: Israel's welfare belongs in David's promise, for a public-spirited king's happiness lies in his people's; 'aforetime' looks back to Egypt.

debates.question: Is the appointing and planting of Israel future or already accomplished?

sides.position: already accomplished — read the verbs as past ('have appointed, have planted')
a settlement now to be perfected as the Canaanites are driven out

schools.summary: Keil: the wordplay carries a deep truth — God must first build a man's house before the man can build God's; the temple waits upon the throne.
Clarke and Wesley: God will build David's family into a sure, continuing house; he is author of our holy purposes and crowns them as our own.
Gill: the parenthesis about Israel closes and the promise returns to David and his posterity.
Ellicott: the promise refers immediately to Solomon; the future tense may show him not yet born, but could be mere assimilation to the passage's futures.
Gill: 'when thy days be fulfilled' means the natural span David exactly filled, living just seventy years.
Wesley: the phrase promises a peaceful death in old age; 'thy seed' is read partly of Solomon, partly of the whole posterity, partly of Christ alone.

debates.question: Does the future tense show that Solomon was not yet born?

sides.position: possibly yes, which would help date the passage
not necessarily — the future may be assimilated to the passage's other futures

schools.summary: Keil: the promise relates primarily to Solomon, while the language of an everlasting throne reaches beyond him.
Gill: Solomon builds the temple, and his antitype the Messiah builds the house of God's true worship.
Clarke and Wesley: the house is Solomon's literally and Christ's ultimately; 'for ever' cannot fit Solomon's ended kingdom but only Christ's.

debates.question: Where does the promise pass from Solomon to Christ?

sides.position: precisely at 'for ever,' which no mortal reign could fulfil
throughout, held in a single typological frame — Solomon the near term, Christ the far

schools.summary: Ellicott: a human, sinning successor is in view, yet the threefold 'for ever' pledges an establishment fulfilled only in the Messiah (cf. Deuteronomy 18:15).
Wesley: the chastening clause fits Solomon and the sinning posterity, not the sinless Christ, so this branch terminates in Solomon; the 'rod of men' is gentle correction.
Gill: God is father to Solomon by adopting grace and to Christ by natural, eternal generation.
Matthew Henry: fatherly chastening with the 'rod of men' is an article of covenant love — what son is not chastened?

debates.question: Does 'if he commit iniquity' apply to Christ?

sides.position: no — it fits only the sinning Solomon and his line
yes, read as passive — 'in his suffering for iniquity,' the Messiah bearing others' guilt

outliers.note: Reads the verb as niphal, translating 'even in his suffering for iniquity,' and refers the chastening to Christ's vicarious sufferings (Isaiah 53).

themes: chastening with the rod of men

schools.summary: Ellicott and Keil: David's line, unlike Saul's, will be punished yet never forgotten, issuing at last in one who conquers sin and death for ever.
Gill: the mercy meant is not inamissible saving grace but the kingdom, a mercy that could be, and was, taken from Saul.
Clarke: David's house lasted to the incarnation, but the verse must not be pressed for unconditional perseverance; Wesley reads the mercy as the promised kingdom.

debates.question: Does the abiding mercy teach unconditional final perseverance?

sides.position: the enduring mercy figures covenant grace to God's own
no — the promise concerns a throne, not personal perseverance

schools.summary: Ellicott: the second 'established' means 'made sure'; David is now head of the line fulfilling the primeval promise (Genesis 3:15) narrowed through Abraham and Judah to his house.
Gill: house and kingdom perpetuated together, begun in Solomon and completed only in the Messiah — a reading Abarbinel too refers to the Messiah's days.
Wesley: 'before thee' — David saw Solomon enthroned as an earnest and, by faith, saw the Messiah's everlasting throne (Psalm 110:1; John 8:56).
Gill: Nathan delivered the whole prophecy exactly, varying nothing — an honest self-correction of the counsel he had given the day before.
Ellicott: 'vision' means any divine communication (cf. Isaiah 1:1); Keil: it was a waking night-vision (chizzayon), distinct from a dream (chalom).
Matthew Henry: David composes himself before the Lord and, though great in every way, asks 'Who am I?' — it becomes the best of men to think lowly of themselves.
Ellicott: David takes the matter before the Lord in the ark's tent; Keil: 'sat' means 'remained/tarried,' not a literal sitting posture of prayer.
Gill: David went into the tabernacle before the ark, confessing his unworthiness of such honour.
Wesley: he entered the tabernacle, sat a while in meditation, then prayed — how unworthy am I and my house of this.

debates.question: Does 'sat before the Lord' mean a literal sitting posture in prayer?

sides.position: no — the verb means 'remained' or 'tarried,' or 'composed himself'
a literal sitting, by a privilege Jewish tradition granted David's kings

schools.summary: Ellicott: the word means 'law,' not 'manner'; read 'this is a law for man!' — wonder at an eternal kingdom promised to such as David. Keil: the law of neighbour-love that God keeps toward David.
Clarke: 'the law of Adam' points to the promised Seed of the woman springing from David's line; Wesley: an astonished 'do men deal so with their inferiors?'
Gill: the past exaltation was but a small thing compared with what God now promises for a great while to come.

debates.question: What does 'is this the manner (law) of man?' mean?

sides.position: 'this is a law for man!' — an exclamation of wonder (not a question)
'the law of man' — the neighbour-love God himself observes toward David
'the law of Adam' — a pointer to the promised Messiah of David's line
a rhetorical question: do men deal thus? No — it is divine grace

schools.summary: Keil: instead of more words, David appeals to God's omniscience, before whom the thankful heart lies open (Psalm 40:9).
Gill: David is at a loss for words in abasement, thanks, and prayer alike; the grants are so large there is no room to ask for more.
Wesley and Clarke: whether in gratitude or petition, words fail; David rests in 'thou knowest thy servant,' sure God knows and will supply his need.
Keil: 'thy word' is an earlier promise — the anointing charge (1 Samuel 16:12) or the older Messianic prophecies (Genesis 49:10; Numbers 24:17).
Gill: 'thy word' is either the promise by Samuel or the Messiah himself, the essential Word (Targum's Memra; LXX 'for thy servant').
Wesley: for thy word's sake — to fulfil thy promise and show thy faithfulness; according to thine own heart — of mere liberality, no desert of mine.
Matthew Henry: all is for the sake of Christ the eternal Word and according to God's good pleasure — 'because it seemed good in thy eyes.'

debates.question: Which 'word' has moved God to do all this?

sides.position: the promise by Samuel / the anointing charge (1 Samuel 16:12)
the Messiah, the essential Word himself
the older Messianic prophecies (Genesis 49:10; Numbers 24:17)

schools.summary: Ellicott: 'heard with our ears' covers the written record too, not mere oral tradition — the whole history of God's dealings (Deuteronomy 4:6).
Gill: God is great in name, nature, persons, perfections, purposes, promises, and works.
Wesley: great in power and goodness alike, as the great and good things done for David prove.
Matthew Henry: honour from God abates no awe; the nearer we are brought to God the more of his glory we see.
Ellicott: 'God went to redeem' takes a plural verb ('whom its gods went to redeem?'); the passage is built on Deuteronomy's language of 'great and terrible things.'
Matthew Henry: Israel's redemption is a type of redemption by Christ; the plural 'Elohim went' hints at the persons of the Godhead, as at creation.
Gill: Israel is unique for the knowledge and worship of the true God, its laws, and its blessings.

outliers.note: Notes the unusual plural verb with 'God,' the textual variant 'by driving out,' and the LXX/Vulgate handling of the difficult 'for you.'

schools.summary: Keil: the establishing covers all God did to make Israel his possession from Moses to this promise, whereby he proved himself their God.
Matthew Henry: the covenant is mutual (a people to thee, a God to them) and immutable, for God who makes it makes it sure.
Wesley: confirmed by promise and covenant and by mighty works; 'their God' by special relation, though he is God of all.
Gill: the bond holds while Israel obeys; forsaking God, they earn a 'Lo-ammi,' as in the end they did.

debates.question: Is Israel's covenant bond simply immutable, or conditioned on obedience?

sides.position: sure and immutable, made good by the God who confirms it
held so long as they obey; forsaking God brings 'Lo-ammi'

schools.summary: Keil: because God promised to build his house, David 'found in his heart' the joy and boldness to pray — the request is the promise turned into a plea.
Gill: David now turns from confession and thanksgiving to petition, asking God to establish the word he has spoken.
Clarke: the promises were conditional; David's unfaithful line lost the throne, so all is fulfilled only in Christ and his spiritual seed.

outliers.note: Digresses on John Haime, the preaching dragoon who believed he never killed a man at Fontenoy — a living parable of why the man of blood was barred from building the temple.

schools.summary: Ellicott: David puts 'hallowed be thy name' first, in the spirit of the Lord's Prayer — living to God's glory was the aim of his whole life.
Matthew Henry: God's glory is the Alpha and Omega of prayer; 'whether I be magnified or no, let thy name be magnified' — as the Son of David also prayed (John 12:28).
Gill: David sought the promise's fulfilment for God's glory, especially in his famous Son the Messiah.
Wesley: a prayer that God would never cease to show himself the God and governor of Israel.
Ellicott: the ground of prayer is always God's promise and lovingkindness; Keil: 'found in his heart' means David found joy and boldness to pray.
Matthew Henry: David durst ask such great things only because God promised them; 'found in his heart' means a heart fixed, not wandering — prayer of the tongue only will not please God.
Gill: David addresses God under the covenant title 'Lord of hosts, God of Israel.'
Keil: the twin 'and now' clauses sum up the prayer, celebrating God's greatness and the truth of his promise.
Gill: 'that God' is the Lord of hosts and God of Israel, who has promised, is able to perform, and is faithful to his word.
Wesley: 'that God' who has declared himself Israel's God and, in particular, David's own God.
Matthew Henry: David builds his faith on the fidelity of the promise — 'thou art that God whose words are true,' one may depend upon.
Ellicott: 'let it please thee' may be optative or a confident future ('it will please thee'), a prophecy founded on the promise (cf. Psalm 110); Keil: the close answers the opening promise.
Gill: David asks the blessing by God's sovereign pleasure, not merit; the Targum's 'begin and bless' pleads for an earnest of the whole.
Wesley: the prayer was abundantly answered when Christ sat down at God's right hand with assurance of an everlasting seed and throne.
Matthew Henry: the entail of the crown finds its perpetuity in Messiah's kingdom, fulfilled when Christ sat down at God's right hand (Hebrews 10:12).

debates.question: Is 'let it please thee' a wish or a confident prophecy?

sides.position: an optative wish, as in the Authorized Version
better a future — 'it will please thee' — a prophecy grounded on the promise

themes: fulfilled in the enthroned Son of David
